# Construct Comparison: GRE Mathematics Subject Test vs. Olympiad-Style Mathematics

## TL;DR
- The two assessments share a thin common core ("mathematical maturity": algebraic fluency, problem decomposition, pattern recognition) but diverge sharply on almost everything each actually scores — the GRE rewards broad recall and fast multiple-choice selection across the undergraduate curriculum; olympiads reward sustained, creative, proof-based reasoning on a few pre-calculus-but-deep problems.
- Verdict on construct overlap: **PARTIAL-to-LOW.** Overlap is real but concentrated in generic reasoning sub-skills; the scored constructs (GRE: speed + breadth + recognition; olympiad: rigorous proof + creativity + stamina) barely intersect.
- The single largest divergence is format-driven: the GRE is a rights-only, 5-option, ~2.6-minute-per-item recognition test, whereas olympiads are open-ended proof constructions graded on logical completeness over hours.

## Key Findings
- **GRE Math Subject Test (verified, ETS):** ~66 five-option multiple-choice questions in 2 hours 50 minutes (~2.6 min/item); ~50% calculus, ~25% algebra, ~25% additional topics. Rights-only scoring. ETS explicitly states the test measures more than recall: the GRE Mathematics Practice Book says "the questions are intended not only to test recall of information, but also to assess the understanding of fundamental concepts and the ability to apply those concepts in various situations."
- **Olympiads (verified, IMO/MAA):** IMO = 6 proof problems over two 4.5-hour days, 7 points each (max 42), partial credit; secondary-school topics (algebra, combinatorics, geometry, number theory), no calculus. Putnam = 12 problems, 10 points each (max 120), proof-based; per the MAA, "beginning this year, the structure of the competition will shift from two three-hour sessions to four ninety-minute sessions, with more frequent designated breaks," with the 2026 exam on Saturday, December 5, 2026.
- **Content overlap is modest:** olympiad number theory, combinatorics, and discrete topics map onto the GRE's "additional topics" (~25%), and algebraic manipulation is shared. But the GRE's dominant calculus half and its abstract/linear-algebra content are largely absent from olympiads, while olympiad geometry and functional equations are largely absent from the GRE.
- **Cognitive divergence:** Under Webb's Depth of Knowledge and Schoenfeld's framework, the GRE concentrates on DOK 1–2 (recall, routine procedures, some multi-step) executed under speed, drawing heavily on "resources"; olympiads concentrate on DOK 3–4 (strategic and extended thinking) and on Schoenfeld's "heuristics," "control," and "beliefs."
- **The word "problem-solving" is treated loosely in popular sources** and conflates two distinct constructs: routine/recognition-based exam problem-solving (GRE) and non-routine creative problem-solving (olympiad).

## Details

### Section A — Side-by-side construct table

| Axis | GRE Mathematics Subject Test | Olympiad (IMO / Putnam / national) |
|---|---|---|
| **Content domain** | Breadth across undergraduate curriculum: ~50% calculus (single/multivariable, sequences/series, ODEs), ~25% algebra (elementary, linear, abstract, number theory), ~25% additional topics (real analysis, discrete math, complex variables, topology, probability/statistics). ETS notes "The percents given are estimates; actual percents will vary somewhat from one edition of the test to another" [high — ETS practice book] | Depth in pre-calculus-but-hard domains: algebra (inequalities, functional equations, polynomials), combinatorics, Euclidean geometry, number theory; deliberately avoids calculus and university-level content [high — IMO/MAA] |
| **Cognitive demand** | Recall + apply fundamental concepts; recognition/selection among 5 options; mostly routine procedures executed accurately; low novel-reasoning demand, no proof [high — ETS + inference] | Novel multi-step reasoning, discovery of hidden structure, proof construction, creativity, tolerance for being stuck [high — MAA/olympiad bodies + math-ed literature] |
| **Item format** | Multiple-choice, 5 options, single best answer; answer-elimination viable; no partial credit but no guessing penalty [high — ETS] | Open-ended written proof; graded 0–7 (IMO) / 0–10 (Putnam) for logical completeness and rigor; partial credit for partial progress (per Wikipedia, "the most frequent scores above zero are 10 points for a complete solution, 9 points for a nearly complete solution, and 1 point for the beginnings of a solution"); no guessing [high — IMO/MAA] |
| **Pacing / time** | ~2.6 min/item across ~66 items; rewards rapid retrieval, triage, "skip-and-return"; speededness is a scored dimension [high — ETS + prep practitioner] | A few problems over many hours (4.5 hr for 3 problems at IMO); rewards sustained deep work; the median Putnam score is usually 0 or 1 out of 120 (Wikipedia: "the median score is usually zero or one point out of 120 possible, and there have been only five perfect scores as of 2021"), signaling extreme difficulty rather than speed [high — MAA/Wikipedia] |
| **Scoring construct** | Number correct → equated scaled score 200–990; rights-only [high — ETS] | Points for rigor/completeness of argument; no reward for an unjustified correct guess [high — IMO/MAA] |

### Section B — Cognitive task analysis (named frameworks)

**GRE Math Subject Test.** Using **Webb's Depth of Knowledge** (Norman Webb), the bulk of GRE items sit at **DOK Level 1 (recall and reproduction: recall a fact/definition or apply a formula) and DOK Level 2 (skills and concepts: more than one mental step, selecting an approach)**, with a minority reaching DOK 3 [moderate — Webb framework applied by inference to ETS item descriptions]. Crucially, ETS's own statement complicates a pure-recall reading: the practice book says "In general, the questions are intended not only to test recall of information, but also to assess the understanding of fundamental concepts and the ability to apply those concepts in various situations" [high — ETS practice book, verbatim], and notes that "questions requiring no more than a good precalculus background may be quite challenging; such questions can be among the most difficult questions on the test" [high — ETS]. Under **Schoenfeld's framework** (resources, heuristics, control, beliefs), GRE success is dominated by **resources** — a broad, fluent, instantly retrievable body of facts and procedures — plus enough **control** to triage items by expected time-cost [moderate — Schoenfeld framework applied by inference]. Heuristics and belief systems are lightly taxed because items are short and recognizable. Under **Bloom's revised taxonomy** (Anderson & Krathwohl), the GRE clusters at **Remember / Understand / Apply**, rarely reaching Analyze/Evaluate and essentially never Create [moderate — Bloom applied by inference].

**Olympiads.** Under **Webb's DOK**, olympiad problems are paradigmatic **DOK Level 3 (strategic thinking; abstract, multi-step, justified) and DOK Level 4 (extended thinking)** [moderate — Webb framework applied]. Under **Schoenfeld**, olympiads heavily tax all four categories but especially **heuristics** ("rules of thumb for making progress in difficult situations"), **control** (deciding when to abandon a line of attack), and **beliefs** (a productive mathematical world-view), with resources necessary but not sufficient [high — Schoenfeld is the canonical framework for exactly this kind of non-routine problem solving]. Under **Bloom**, olympiads reach **Analyze, Evaluate, and Create** — the construction of an original proof is a "Create"-level act [moderate — Bloom applied]. **Mason, Burton & Stacey's** "Thinking Mathematically" adds the processes most distinctive to olympiads: **specializing, generalizing, conjecturing, and convincing** — the last (rigorous justification) being exactly what the GRE format cannot elicit [high — Mason/Burton/Stacey].

### Section C — Overlap vs. divergence

**Shared sub-skills (genuine construct overlap):**
- Algebraic manipulation fluency (factoring, identities, symbol pushing) [high]
- Comfort decomposing an unfamiliar problem into sub-steps [moderate]
- Pattern recognition / recognizing applicable structure or theorem [moderate]
- Number-theory and combinatorics content knowledge (maps to GRE "additional topics") [moderate]
- General "mathematical maturity" / fluency with abstraction [moderate — this is the steelman skeptic's "only" overlap]

**GRE-specific skills olympiads rarely train:**
- Broad calculus recall and execution (~50% of GRE; absent from olympiads) [high]
- Linear and abstract algebra content recall (eigenvalues, group theory, ring theory) [high]
- Multiple-choice technique: answer elimination, back-solving from options, plugging in [high — inference from format]
- Speed / rapid retrieval and per-item triage under a ~2.6-min budget [high]
- Real analysis / topology / complex-variables / probability recognition items [high]

**Olympiad-specific skills the GRE never tests:**
- Rigorous proof construction and written mathematical communication [high]
- Sustained reasoning over hours on a single problem; stamina for being "stuck" [high]
- Creative invention of a non-obvious method (substitution, coloring argument, invariant) [high]
- Euclidean geometry proof and functional-equation technique [high]
- Conjecturing and convincing in the Mason/Burton/Stacey sense [high]

### Section D — Construct-level prior

Based purely on what each instrument measures (not on any transfer evidence): olympiad practice plausibly *touches* a minority of GRE-relevant skills — chiefly algebraic fluency, problem decomposition, pattern recognition, and discrete/number-theory content that appears in the GRE's 25% "additional topics" band. But the constructs the GRE actually *scores* — broad calculus recall, multiple-choice selection, and speed — are largely orthogonal to what olympiads cultivate, while the construct olympiads most distinctively measure (rigorous proof) is one the GRE format structurally cannot test. The honest construct-level prior is therefore **partial-to-low overlap**: the assessments share a generic reasoning substrate but diverge on the specific, scored performances. A skeptic's framing — that the overlap reduces to "mathematical maturity" while the GRE's scored variance is dominated by breadth, speed, and format technique — is well supported by the construct analysis and should be taken seriously.

### Steelmanning the skeptic & the "problem-solving" equivocation
The strongest skeptical case: psychometric research on **test speededness** shows that scores on time-limited tests routinely decompose into two latent factors — a "level/ability" factor and a separate "speed" factor unrelated to the target ability (Davison and colleagues; reviewed by Lu & Sireci) — implying that part of GRE variance reflects retrieval speed and triage rather than deep reasoning, shrinking the construct overlap with untimed olympiad work. Relatedly, the **AIME-vs-USAMO "proof gap"** demonstrates within competition math that answer-only formats (like AIME, and by extension the GRE's MC format) measure a different construct than proof formats: per Petrov et al., "Proof or Bluff? Evaluating LLMs on 2025 USA Math Olympiad" (arXiv:2503.21934), "all tested models struggled significantly, achieving less than 5% on average" on USAMO proofs, despite AIME scores "comparable to top human competitors" (a later revision notes only Gemini-2.5-Pro reached a non-trivial 25%). This is direct evidence that format changes the measured construct. Finally, the literature distinguishes **routine/mechanical problem solving** from **creative non-routine problem solving**; conflating them inflates apparent overlap. Fields Medalist Terence Tao cautions, verbatim, that "mathematical competitions are very different activities from mathematical learning or mathematical research; don't expect the problems you get in, say, graduate study, to have the same cut-and-dried, neat flavour that an Olympiad problem does," though Evan Chen and others push back on an over-strong version of that mantra — experts disagree on how distinct competition skill is from broader mathematical ability, and that disagreement is itself a caveat.

## Recommendations
*(Scope note: the task explicitly excludes transfer, sequencing, and effect-size claims; these are descriptive recommendations for how to use this construct map.)*
1. **Treat the overlap as concentrated, not diffuse.** When reasoning about the two assessments, attribute shared ground specifically to algebraic fluency, problem decomposition, pattern recognition, and discrete/number-theory content — not to a vague "both are math."
2. **Tag every cross-walk by construct, not topic.** A shared topic label (e.g., "number theory") can mask a format-driven construct gap (recognition item vs. proof). Always specify whether you mean content, cognitive level (DOK/Bloom), or format.
3. **Hold speed and proof as the two poles.** If a future analysis revisits this, the benchmarks that would change the verdict from "partial-low" toward "partial-high" are: evidence that GRE variance is *not* dominated by speededness (a small speed factor), and evidence that GRE items cluster at DOK 3 rather than DOK 1–2. Conversely, confirmation of strong speededness or heavy DOK-1 loading would push the verdict toward "low."

## Caveats
- "~66 questions" and the topic percentages are ETS's own estimates; ETS states "The percents given are estimates; actual percents will vary somewhat from one edition of the test to another" [high — ETS].
- DOK/Bloom level assignments for whole assessments are **inferences** applying published frameworks to ETS/olympiad item descriptions, not findings from a published item-by-item coding study; treat as moderate-confidence.
- The Putnam format is changing in 2026 (the MAA states it will "shift from two three-hour sessions to four ninety-minute sessions"), which slightly alters its pacing construct but not its proof-based nature [high — MAA].
- Some content/scoring details were corroborated via test-prep practitioners (Kaplan, Magoosh) rather than ETS; these are flagged in-text and are lower confidence than ETS primary sources. One practitioner page asserted a "1/4-point per wrong answer" penalty on a paper version — this contradicts ETS's own rights-only statement ("Nothing is subtracted from a score if you answer a question incorrectly") and is judged erroneous.

## Self-confidence summary
High confidence on all format/content/scoring facts (ETS, IMO, MAA primary sources). Moderate confidence on the cognitive-framework mappings, which are principled inferences rather than empirical codings. **One-line verdict: construct overlap is PARTIAL — real at the level of generic mathematical-reasoning sub-skills but low on everything each test actually scores — and the single most important caveat is that the GRE's multiple-choice, speeded, rights-only format measures a fundamentally different performance (fast recognition) than the olympiad's open-ended proof construction.**

## Sources
**Institutional / primary (test makers):**
- ETS, "Practice Book for the GRE® Subject Test in Mathematics" (2024, Form GR3768), https://www.ets.org/pdfs/gre/practice-book-math.pdf — content percentages, ~66 items, 2h50m, construct statement, rights-only scoring ("Nothing is subtracted from a score if you answer a question incorrectly").
- ETS, "GRE Subject Test Content and Structure," https://www.ets.org/gre/test-takers/subject-tests/about/content-structure.html
- ETS, "GRE Subject Test Strategies and Tips," https://www.ets.org/gre/test-takers/subject-tests/prepare/strategies.html
- ETS, "GRE Mathematics Subject Test (For Test Takers)" content page, https://origin-www.ets.org/gre/subject/about/content/mathematics/
- International Mathematical Olympiad, "What is IMO?," https://www.imo-official.org/about/ and General Regulations (2025), https://www.imo-official.org/documents/RegulationsIMO.pdf
- Mathematical Association of America, "MAA Putnam," https://maa.org/putnam/ — format and 2026 change to four 90-minute sessions; MAA Putnam Archive, https://maa.org/maa-putnam-archive/

**Peer-reviewed / academic:**
- Schoenfeld, A. H., "Mathematical Problem Solving" (Academic Press, 1985) — resources/heuristics/control/beliefs framework.
- Mason, J., Burton, L., & Stacey, K., "Thinking Mathematically" (1982; 2nd ed. 2010, Pearson) — specializing/generalizing/conjecturing/convincing.
- Anderson, L. W., & Krathwohl, D. R. (eds.), "A Taxonomy for Learning, Teaching, and Assessing: A Revision of Bloom's Taxonomy of Educational Objectives" (Longman, 2001) — revised Bloom's taxonomy.
- Webb, N. L., "Criteria for Alignment of Expectations and Assessments in Mathematics and Science Education" (CCSSO Research Monograph No. 6, 1997) — Depth of Knowledge.
- Petrov et al., "Proof or Bluff? Evaluating LLMs on 2025 USA Math Olympiad," arXiv:2503.21934, https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.21934 — answer-only vs. proof construct gap.
- Davison, Semmes, Huang & Close (2012); Lu & Sireci (2007), "Validity Issues in Test Speededness," on speededness as a separable latent factor (via ScienceDirect reviews of standardized-intelligence speed/level separation).
- "Perspectives on mathematics competitions and their relationship with mathematics education," ZDM – Mathematics Education (2022), https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11858-022-01404-z

**Named experts / practitioners:**
- Terence Tao, "Advice on mathematics competitions," https://terrytao.wordpress.com/career-advice/advice-on-mathematics-competitions/
- Evan Chen, "Against the 'Research vs. Olympiads' Mantra," https://blog.evanchen.cc/2016/08/13/against-the-research-vs-olympiads-mantra/
- Kiran Kedlaya, The Putnam Archive, https://kskedlaya.org/putnam-archive/
- "International Mathematical Olympiad" and "William Lowell Putnam Mathematical Competition," Wikipedia (format, scoring distribution, median-score statistic) — corroborating primary-source details.
- Kaplan and Magoosh GRE prep pages (pacing/format corroboration; lower confidence; one Kaplan/practitioner penalty claim judged erroneous against ETS).
